title: OpenAI Responses API WebSocket Mode Guide: Persistent Connections for Agentic Workflows (2026)
notes: OpenAI launched WebSocket mode for the Responses API — persistent connections for agentic workflows that eliminate per-request connection overhead. Key benefit: up to ~40% faster end-to-end execution for workflows with 20+ tool calls. Additionally: server-side compaction (set context_management with compact_threshold — server auto-compacts when threshold crossed, no separate /responses/compact call needed). Together these features make the Responses API the right choice for long-running agents that previously choked on latency. title: Agentic RAG Production Guide: Adaptive Routing, Multi-Hop Queries, and LangGraph Patterns (2026)
notes: Agentic RAG extends standard RAG by adding a reasoning loop: the agent decides whether to retrieve, what to retrieve, whether the result is relevant, whether to re-query, and when to stop. In 2026, naive RAG handles 80% of production use cases; Agentic RAG is warranted for multi-hop questions, fact-checking, and research synthesis. Core patterns: (1) ReAct RAG — agent reasons then acts (retrieve), iterates up to N times; (2) FLARE (Forward-Looking Active Retrieval) — predicts upcoming context needs and retrieves proactively; (3) Self-RAG — model generates retrieve tokens during generation, enabling fine-grained selective retrieval; (4) Corrective RAG (CRAG) — evaluates retrieved docs, uses web search as fallback for low-confidence retrievals. Adaptive RAG: query classifier routes to naive RAG (simple factoid), Advanced RAG (multi-chunk reasoning), or Agentic RAG (complex multi-hop) based on query complexity — best production strategy for cost control. LangGraph is the most mature framework for production agentic RAG loops (built-in checkpointing, human-in-the-loop, time-travel debugging). Developer guide: decision framework (when to use each pattern), LangGraph Agentic RAG implementation, RAGAS evaluation for agentic RAG, cost model (agentic loops can be 3-10x more expensive than standard RAG), combining with GraphRAG for relationship-dense corpora. KD 7, SV 320.

title: LLM Quantization Guide 2026: GGUF vs GPTQ vs AWQ vs bitsandbytes for Production
keyword: llm quantization guide gguf gptq awq 2026
notes: LLM quantization has matured into a clear production decision framework in 2026. Four formats dominate: (1) GGUF — llama.cpp/Ollama format, CPU+GPU hybrid inference, Q4_K_M sweet spot (~4x VRAM reduction, <2% quality loss), best for developer workstations and single-user deployment; (2) AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization) — default INT4 for production inference, selects 1% most important weights for higher precision, optimized kernels in vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM (741 tok/s with Marlin kernel), most model families ship pre-quantized AWQ on HuggingFace; (3) GPTQ — first method to achieve 4-bit with accuracy, supported in vLLM/HF Transformers/text-gen-webui, extensive pre-quantized model library; (4) bitsandbytes — the ONLY option supporting training (QLoRA), slower inference than AWQ/GPTQ. Decision framework: workstation/Ollama → GGUF; production multi-user inference → AWQ + vLLM; training/fine-tuning → bitsandbytes QLoRA; existing GPTQ ecosystem → GPTQ on vLLM. Also covers FP8 (NVIDIA H100/H200 native, MiMo V2.5 uses MXFP8 training), NVFP4 (new in 2026), EXL2 (ExLlamaV2 format, better accuracy than GPTQ at same bit depth). Q4_K_M is the 2026 sweet spot: 4x VRAM reduction with <2% quality loss. Developer guide: format selection decision tree, loading each format (Python code), benchmarks at each bit depth, combining quantization with LoRA fine-tuning (QLoRA patterns), cost calculator for GPU requirements. KD 7, SV 350.

title: DSPy Developer Guide: Programmatic Prompt Optimization for Production AI Systems (2026)
notes: DSPy (Stanford NLP) is the 'programming rather than prompting' framework — instead of writing prompts manually, you define your pipeline as Python functions and let DSPy optimize the prompts through compilation (100-500 LLM calls upfront for 99%+ runtime reliability). Key for production: when you update models (GPT-4o → Claude Sonnet 4.6), DSPy re-optimizes automatically without rewriting prompts. MIPROv2 optimizer: bootstraps traces across inputs, filters high-scoring trajectories, optimizes both instructions and few-shot examples. Pipeline definition: dspy.Module subclass → define forward() → call self.predict/self.chain_of_thought → compile with optimizer. Where DSPy wins vs manual prompts: consistent output structure across model versions, measurable optimization (objective metric), cross-model portability, automated prompt iteration (no human judgment needed). DSPy 2.28 supports passing agent State directly to tools without extra wiring. Use cases: high-reliability classification (99%+ required), enterprise pipelines that must run across model providers, teams without dedicated prompt engineers. Developer guide: DSPy setup and first pipeline, Module definition patterns, signature syntax (typed I/O), MIPROv2 optimizer configuration, evaluation metric definition, compiling and saving optimized programs, deploying optimized DSPy modules (serialization, model swapping), combining with LangGraph for complex agentic flows.
